article_title: Meta Breaks Ground on $1 Billion AI Data Center in Tulsa as City Moratorium Exempts 'Project Anthem'

content_preview.title: Meta Breaks Ground on $1 Billion AI Data Center in Tulsa as City Moratorium Exempts 'Project Anthem'
content_preview.summary: Meta's first Oklahoma data center — a 2-million-square-foot AI facility in East Tulsa — was grandfathered past a city moratorium that paused similar projects, drawing local protests.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Meta broke ground on April 21, 2026 on a $1 billion AI-optimized data center in East Tulsa, Oklahoma — the company's first facility in the state, its 28th in the United States, and its 32nd globally. Known internally as Project Anthem, the 2-million-square-foot campus at Fair Oaks Innovation Park is slated for completion in 2028, according to [Meta's official announcement](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/breaking-ground-new-ai-optimized-data-center-tulsa-oklahoma/). The groundbrea...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent 834-word News piece. Thorough coverage of the groundbreaking, the moratorium exemption angle, local protest, and unresolved policy questions (SB 1488 state bill, water offset accounting). The 'What We Don't Know' section raises genuinely important questions about the facility's power draw and whether clean energy commitments are additive.
editor_notes.source_verification: Meta FB announcement: fetched — $1B+ ✓, 28th US / 32nd global ✓, 1500MW clean energy ✓, liquid cooling zero water ✓; 2M sq ft and Project Anthem not in this source (confirmed by KRMG and Tulsa Flyer respectively). Tulsa Flyer: fetched — moratorium exemption ✓, Councilor Bengel NDA ✓, $3.3B economic impact ✓, 85% tax exemption / $36M over 25 years ✓, Stop Data Colonialism / Cheyenna Morgan protesting water claims ✓, 340-acre site ✓, 2028 completion ✓; note: Tulsa Flyer source describes Morgan holding a sign reading 'Meta profits billions off destruction of our community' and criticizing 'water-neutrality claims as misleading narratives' — the article's 'a lie' in quotes was not verbatim in the WebFetch result, but characterization is consistent. Oklahoma Commerce: fetched — Governor Stitt quote ✓, REDI certification at Fair Oaks ✓, Gary Demasi quote ✓. KRMG: fetched — 1000+ construction workers ✓, 100+ permanent staff ✓, Fortis Construction ✓, 2M sq ft ✓. Meta Data Centers blog: fetched — Phytech partnership ✓, 1500 acres farmland ✓, 50M gallons water savings ✓, corn/soybeans/winter wheat ✓, $25M infrastructure ✓, water-positive 2030 ✓.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified across five sources. Minor advisory: Morgan's 'a lie' appears in quotes in the body but WebFetch of Tulsa Flyer describes her as criticizing water claims as 'misleading narratives' (and a sign quote) rather than 'a lie' verbatim — may be in full article text. Core facts solid throughout.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Balanced. The article includes both Meta's economic and environmental claims and the protest/counter-narrative without editorial bias. The 'What We Don't Know' section on water accounting and SB 1488 is excellent investigative framing.
editor_notes.originality: Prior Machine Herald coverage: Indiana data center (February 12) referenced internally. Oklahoma/Tulsa-specific story is new. State data center moratorium context from March 6 article also referenced. No duplicate.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality local news story on the Meta Tulsa groundbreaking with strong investigative context on the moratorium exemption and water controversy. All five sources verified and correctly attributed. Ready for publication.
